items.q: What winter sport features players sliding heavy stones toward a target while teammates sweep the ice?
items.explanation: Curling originated in Scotland, and Olympic teams score by placing stones closest to the center of the house.

items.q: Venus is the hottest planet in the Solar System.
items.explanation: Venus stays hotter than Mercury because its dense carbon dioxide atmosphere traps heat very effectively.

items.q: What comic-strip character is famous for pulling the football away from Charlie Brown?
items.explanation: Lucy van Pelt is one of the central Peanuts characters created by Charles M. Schulz.

items.q: What red liquid carries oxygen around the human body?
items.explanation: Red blood cells contain hemoglobin, the protein that binds oxygen and helps transport it through the body.

items.q: Which sport uses a bow to shoot arrows at a target?
items.explanation: Archery has been practiced for hunting and warfare for thousands of years and remains an Olympic sport.

items.q: What title was given to the rulers of ancient Egypt?
items.explanation: The term pharaoh originally referred to the royal palace before becoming a title for the king of Egypt.

items.q: Braille is designed to be read by touch.
items.explanation: Standard Braille characters are arranged in cells made from up to six raised dots.

items.q: What toy is flown on a string in the wind?
items.explanation: Kites have been used for recreation, signaling, and scientific experiments in many cultures for centuries.

items.q: What baked dessert is traditionally topped with candles on birthdays?
items.explanation: Birthday cakes appear in many cultures, and candles are often linked to wishes made before blowing them out.

items.q: What object with teeth is used to untangle or arrange hair?
items.explanation: Combs have been made from wood, bone, metal, and plastic in cultures around the world.

items.q: In Roman mythology, who is the counterpart of the Greek god Ares?
items.explanation: Mars was associated with war and also held an important place in Roman state religion.

items.q: The Mercator projection preserves local compass bearings.
items.explanation: That property made Mercator maps useful for navigation, even though they greatly distort area near the poles.

items.q: What legal protection shields diplomats from prosecution under the host country's ordinary laws?
items.explanation: Diplomatic immunity is grounded in international agreements such as the Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations.

items.q: What dramatic term describes a speech a character delivers alone on stage to reveal thoughts?
items.explanation: Soliloquies are a well-known feature of many Shakespeare plays and can reveal motives or inner conflict directly to the audience.

items.q: Which playwright wrote Hamlet?
items.explanation: Hamlet is one of Shakespeare's major tragedies and centers on the prince of Denmark.

items.q: Which scientist introduced the concept of natural selection as a mechanism of evolution?
items.explanation: Darwin presented natural selection most famously in On the Origin of Species, published in 1859.

items.q: A leap second is added to UTC to keep it close to Earth's rotation.
items.explanation: Leap seconds are inserted irregularly because Earth's rotation is not perfectly uniform.

items.q: What style of architecture is associated with rounded arches and thick masonry walls in medieval Europe?
items.explanation: Romanesque churches often used barrel vaults and massive walls before Gothic architecture developed taller forms and pointed arches.

items.q: What art term describes strong contrast between light and dark used to model form?
items.explanation: Renaissance and Baroque artists used chiaroscuro to give paintings and drawings a dramatic sense of volume.

items.q: In Norse mythology, what is the name of the rainbow bridge leading to Asgard?
items.explanation: Bifrost links the world of humans with the realm of the gods and is guarded by Heimdall in many tellings.

items.q: What writ orders a public official or lower court to perform a required duty?
items.explanation: A writ of mandamus commands the performance of an obligation that the law treats as mandatory rather than discretionary.

items.q: A finite group of prime order is always cyclic.
items.explanation: In such a group, every non-identity element generates the whole group because its order must divide the group's prime size.

items.q: What term describes the philosophical view that only one's own mind is certain to exist?
items.explanation: Solipsism pushes skepticism to an extreme by questioning whether any external world or other minds can be known.

items.q: Which Persian king founded Persepolis as a ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid Empire?
items.explanation: Darius began Persepolis in the late 6th century BCE, and later Achaemenid rulers expanded the complex.

items.q: Which medieval theologian wrote Summa Theologica?
items.explanation: Aquinas composed the work as a systematic presentation of theology, philosophy, and moral reasoning.

items.q: What term describes a positive integer equal to the sum of its proper divisors?
items.explanation: The smallest perfect number is 6 because its proper divisors 1, 2, and 3 add up to 6.

items.q: A valid argument can have false premises and a false conclusion.
items.explanation: Validity concerns whether the conclusion follows from the premises, not whether the premises or conclusion are actually true.

items.q: Which architect is associated with the phrase Less is more?
items.explanation: Mies used the phrase to express a minimalist approach that shaped much of twentieth-century modernist architecture.

items.q: Which church council resolved the Western Schism in 1417 by electing Pope Martin V?
items.explanation: The council met from 1414 to 1418 and helped end a period in which rival popes claimed authority.

items.q: Which fourteenth-century story collection is framed around young people sheltering from the Black Death outside Florence?
items.explanation: Giovanni Boccaccio organized The Decameron as one hundred stories told over ten days.
